html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 剪燈新話 - Title of the collection, translating to 'New Tales of Cutting the Lamp.'
  • 懷才不遇 - Means 'remained unrecognized despite his talent throughout his life.'
  • 蒙禍 - Means 'suffered misfortune' or 'was implicated in a disaster,' likely referring to a specific political incident.
  • 被貶謫十年 - Means 'was exiled for ten years.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 瞿佑 - A real historical figure, a Ming dynasty writer known for his contributions to Chinese literature.
  • 錢塘（今浙江杭州） - Refers to Hangzhou, a city in Zhejiang Province, which was historically known as Qiantang.
  • 明初著名文學家 - Refers to the early Ming dynasty, a period of cultural and literary flourishing in China.
  • 洪武年間 - The reign of the first Ming emperor, Zhu Yuanzhang, who ruled from 1368 to 1398.
  • 永樂年間 - The reign of the third Ming emperor, Zhu Di, who ruled from 1402 to 1424.
  • 《剪燈新話》 - A collection of short stories that became influential in Chinese literature, particularly in the genre of 'tales of the supernatural.'

Proper Nouns

  • 瞿佑 - The name of the author.
  • 宗吉 - The courtesy name of the author.
  • 存齋 - The artistic name of the author.
  • 錢塘（今浙江杭州） - The place of origin of the author, now known as Hangzhou.
  • 洪武年間 - The reign of the first Ming emperor, Zhu Yuanzhang.
  • 永樂年間 - The reign of the third Ming emperor, Zhu Di.
  • 《剪燈新話》 - The title of the author's collection of short stories.

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 既编辑古今怪奇之事 - Having compiled strange and marvelous events from ancient and modern times.
  • 以为《剪灯录》 - To create the *Jian Deng Lu* (Record of Lamp Cutting).
  • 好事者每以近事相闻 - Those fond of such tales often share recent events.
  • 远不出百年，近止在数载 - Ranging from not more than a century ago to as recent as a few years.
  • 襞积于中，日新月盛 - Accumulating in my mind, becoming more abundant each day.
  • 习气所溺，欲罢不能 - Habitual fascination, making it impossible to stop.
  • 乃援笔为文以纪之 - I then took up my pen to record them.
  • 其事皆可喜可悲，可惊可怪者 - The events are all joyful, sorrowful, surprising, and strange.
  • 所惜笔路荒芜，词源浅狭 - The only regret is that my writing is barren and my vocabulary limited.
  • 无嵬目鸿耳之论以发扬之耳 - There is no profound insight to elevate it.
  • 既成，又自以为涉于语怪，近于海淫 - Once completed, I felt it delved into the realm of strange tales, verging on the bizarre.
  • 藏之书笥，不欲传出 - I kept it in a chest, not wanting it to be circulated.
  • 客闻而求观者众，不能尽却之 - Many visitors heard about it and wanted to see it, and I couldn't refuse them all.
  • 《诗》、《书》、《易》、《春秋》、皆圣笔之所述作 - The *Shijing*, *Shujing*, *Yijing*, and *Chunqiu* are all works of the sages.
  • 以为万世大经大法者也 - Considered the great principles and laws for all ages.
  • 然而《易》言龙战于野，《书》载雉雊于鼎，《国风》取淫奔之诗，《春秋》纪乱贼之事 - However, the *Yijing* speaks of dragons fighting in the wild, the *Shujing* records a rooster crowing from a tripod, the *Guofeng* includes poems about licentiousness, and the *Chunqiu* documents chaotic and rebellious events.
  • 是又不可执一论也 - These cannot be judged by a single criterion.
  • 今余此编，虽于世教民彝，莫之或补 - My compilation, though it may not contribute much to the norms of society and morality.
  • 而劝善惩恶，哀穷悼屈，其亦庶乎言者无罪，闻者足以戒之一义云尔 - It still serves to encourage good and punish evil, to pity the poor and mourn the oppressed, and it aligns with the principle that 'speakers should not be punished, and listeners should be warned.'
  • 客以余言有理，故书之卷首 - The visitors found my reasoning sound, so I wrote it at the beginning of the volume.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 《剪灯录》 - The title of the work being discussed, *Jian Deng Lu* (Record of Lamp Cutting).
  • 《诗》、《书》、《易》、《春秋》 - The *Shijing* (Book of Poetry), *Shujing* (Book of Documents), *Yijing* (Book of Changes), and *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals), which are part of the Five Classics.
  • 《国风》 - A section of the *Shijing* that contains folk songs and poetry.
  • 圣笔之所述作 - Refers to the works of the sages, emphasizing their authority and wisdom.
  • 言者无罪，闻者足以戒 - A principle from ancient Chinese thought, meaning that speakers should not be punished for their words, but listeners should be warned by them.

Proper Nouns

  • 《剪灯录》 - The title of the work being discussed.
  • 《诗》、《书》、《易》、《春秋》 - The *Shijing* (Book of Poetry), *Shujing* (Book of Documents), *Yijing* (Book of Changes), and *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals).
  • 《国风》 - A section of the *Shijing*.
  • 圣笔之所述作 - Refers to the works of the sages.
  • 言者无罪，闻者足以戒 - A principle from ancient Chinese thought.

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 洪武十一年岁次戊午六月朔日 - Formal date expression meaning 'the first day of June in the year of Wuwu (戊午) of the 11th year of the Hongwu era.'
  • 山阳瞿佑书于吴山大隐堂 - Formal statement indicating the place and date of writing: 'Qü You of Shanyang wrote this in the Great Hidden Hall on Mount Wu.'
  • 昔陈鸿作《长恨传》并《东城老父传》 - Reference to the works of Chen Hong, meaning 'In the past, Chen Hong wrote *The Story of Eternal Regret* and *The Tale of the Old Man from the Eastern City*.'
  • 时人称其史才，咸推许之 - Common expression meaning 'people at the time praised his historical talent and all commended him.'
  • 牛憎孺之《幽怪录》 - Reference to the work of Niu Zengru, meaning 'Niu Zengru's *Records of the Mysterious and Strange*.'
  • 刘斧之《青琐集》 - Reference to the work of Liu Fu, meaning 'Liu Fu's *Collection of the Green Lock*.'
  • 述奇纪异 - Phrase meaning 'to narrate strange and extraordinary events.'
  • 其事之有无不必论 - Phrase meaning 'whether the events are true or not need not be discussed.'
  • 其制作之体，则亦工矣 - Phrase meaning 'the structure of the work is also well-crafted.'
  • 宗吉之志确而勤 - Phrase meaning 'Zongji's determination is firm and diligent.'
  • 故其学也博 - Phrase meaning 'therefore, his learning is extensive.'
  • 具才充而敏 - Phrase meaning 'his talent is abundant and quick.'
  • 故其文也赡 - Phrase meaning 'therefore, his writing is rich and abundant.'
  • 是编虽稗官之流 - Phrase meaning 'this collection, though a work of minor officials, is still valuable.'
  • 劝善惩恶，动存鉴戒 - Phrase meaning 'it encourages good and punishes evil, always serving as a moral lesson.'
  • 不可谓无补于世 - Phrase meaning 'it cannot be said to be without benefit to the world.'
  • 矧夫造意之奇，措词之妙 - Phrase meaning 'especially the ingenious conception and the exquisite choice of words.'
  • 粲然自成一家言 - Phrase meaning 'they shine brightly, forming a unique style of their own.'
  • 读之使人喜而手舞足蹈，悲而掩卷堕泪者 - Phrase meaning 'reading it makes one joyful and dancing with delight, or sad and shedding tears.'
  • 盖亦有之 - Phrase meaning 'there are indeed such cases.'
  • 自非好古博雅，工于文而审于事 - Phrase meaning 'unless one is fond of antiquity, erudite, skilled in writing, and discerning in matters.'
  • 曷能臻此哉 - Phrase meaning 'how could one achieve this?'
  • 《秋香亭记》之作 - Reference to the work 'Record of the Autumn Fragrance Pavilion.'
  • 余将质之宗吉 - Phrase meaning 'I will discuss this with Zongji.'
  • 不知果然否 - Phrase meaning 'I wonder if it is truly so.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 洪武十一年 - The 11th year of the Hongwu era (1378) in the Ming Dynasty.
  • 戊午 - The heavenly stem and earthly branch for the year 1378.
  • 山阳 - A place name, possibly referring to a region in modern-day Jiangsu Province.
  • 瞿佑 - A Ming Dynasty writer known for his work *New Tales Under the Lamp*.
  • 陈鸿 - A Tang Dynasty writer known for *The Story of Eternal Regret* and *The Tale of the Old Man from the Eastern City*.
  • 牛憎孺 - A Tang Dynasty writer known for *Records of the Mysterious and Strange*.
  • 刘斧 - A Song Dynasty writer known for *Collection of the Green Lock*.
  • 稗官之流 - A term referring to minor officials who write, often used to describe minor literary works.
  • 劝善惩恶 - A Confucian principle of encouraging good and punishing evil.
  • 动存鉴戒 - A phrase meaning 'always serving as a moral lesson.'

Proper Nouns

  • 洪武 - The era name of the Ming Dynasty, referring to the reign of Emperor Hongwu.
  • 瞿佑 - The author of *New Tales Under the Lamp*.
  • 陈鸿 - A Tang Dynasty writer.
  • 牛憎孺 - A Tang Dynasty writer.
  • 刘斧 - A Song Dynasty writer.
  • 《剪灯新话》 - The title of the work being discussed.
  • 《长恨传》 - A work by Chen Hong.
  • 《东城老父传》 - Another work by Chen Hong.
  • 《幽怪录》 - A work by Niu Zengru.
  • 《青琐集》 - A work by Liu Fu.
  • 《秋香亭记》 - A work by Qü You.

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 洪武三十年夏四月 - Specific date in the Ming dynasty, indicating the time of writing.
  • 钱塘凌云翰序 - The title of the preface, indicating the author and the location.
  • 《剪灯新话》序三 - The title of the third preface to the novel *Jian Deng Xin Hua*.
  • 宗吉先生 - A respectful title for the author of *Jian Deng Xin Hua*.
  • 词则传奇之流 - Indicates that the style of the work is similar to that of legends or tales.
  • 意则子氏之寓言也 - Suggests that the meaning of the work is akin to allegorical stories.
  • 家学渊源 - Refers to a family tradition of learning.
  • 博及群集 - Means having extensive knowledge across various fields.
  • 屡荐明经 - Indicates being repeatedly recommended for official positions based on Confucian learning.
  • 母老不仕 - Suggests the author chose not to take up official duties due to his mother's age.
  • 肆力于文学 - Means dedicating oneself to literature.
  • 接其论议 - Refers to engaging in discussions with the author.
  • 观其著述 - Means reading the author's works.
  • 开武库 - A metaphor for opening a vast collection of military knowledge.
  • 游宝坊 - A metaphor for exploring a treasure trove of knowledge.
  • 惊人之奇 - Refers to astonishing and rare things.
  • 希世之珍 - Means rare treasures of the world.
  • 特武库、室坊中之一耳 - Suggests that this work is just one item among many in the vast collection.
  • 观是编者，于宗吉之学之博，尚有愆也 - Implies that readers who only see this work might not fully appreciate the breadth of the author's knowledge.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 洪武三十年 - The 30th year of the Hongwu era (1397) during the Ming dynasty.
  • 钱塘 - Refers to Hangzhou, a city in Zhejiang province.
  • 凌云翰 - The name of the author of the preface.
  • 《剪灯新话》 - A famous collection of short stories written by Wang Jiegeng during the Ming dynasty.
  • 宗吉先生 - A respectful title for Wang Jiegeng.
  • 传奇之流 - Refers to the genre of legends or tales, which was popular in Ming literature.
  • 子氏之寓言 - Refers to the style of allegorical stories, often associated with the works of Zhuangzi.
  • 洪武十四年 - The 14th year of the Hongwu era (1381) during the Ming dynasty.
  • 吴植 - The name of the author of the second preface.
  • 钱塘邑庠进德斋 - Refers to a school or academy in Hangzhou where the second preface was written.

Proper Nouns

  • 洪武 - The era name of the first Ming emperor, Zhu Yuanzhang.
  • 钱塘 - The old name for Hangzhou.
  • 凌云翰 - The name of the author of the first preface.
  • 《剪灯新话》 - The title of the novel.
  • 宗吉先生 - A respectful title for Wang Jiegeng.
  • 吴植 - The name of the author of the second preface.
  • 钱塘邑庠进德斋 - The name of the school or academy where the second preface was written.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 《剪灯新话》 - Title of the work being discussed, which translates to 'New Tales of the Lamp Snipping.'
  • 序四 - The fourth preface, indicating the specific section of the text.
  • 余观昌黎韩子作《毛颖传》 - 'I observed that the Master Han of Changli wrote *The Biography of Mao Ying*,' where *毛颖传* is a famous work by Han Yu, a prominent Tang dynasty writer.
  • 柳子厚读而奇之 - 'Liu Zihou read it and found it extraordinary,' referring to Liu Zongyuan, another renowned Tang dynasty writer.
  • 古之文人，其相推奖类若此 - 'Ancient writers would praise each other in this manner,' highlighting the mutual respect among literary figures.
  • 《谪龙说》与《河间传》等 - 'The *Exiled Dragon* and *The Biography of the River Intendant*,' two works by Liu Zongyuan.
  • 后之人亦未闻有以妄且淫病子厚者 - 'Later generations have not heard of anyone criticizing Liu Zongyuan for being frivolous or licentious,' suggesting that Liu's work was well-received despite its unconventional nature.
  • 岂前辈所见，有不逮今耶 - 'Is it that the vision of our predecessors was not as sharp as that of today?' A rhetorical question implying that modern critics may be less discerning.
  • 亦忠厚之志焉耳矣 - 'It is also a reflection of their sincere and generous spirit,' emphasizing the positive intentions behind Liu's work.
  • 余友瞿宗吉之为《剪灯新话》 - 'My friend Qu Zongji composed *New Tales of the Lamp Snipping*,' introducing the author and his work.
  • 其所志怪，有过于马孺子所言 - 'The strange tales he recorded are even more fantastic than those told by Ma Ruzi,' comparing Qu's work to that of another author.
  • 而淫则无若河间之甚者 - 'But in terms of licentiousness, none surpasses the *River Intendant*,' again referencing Liu Zongyuan's work.
  • 而或者犹沾沾然置噱于其间 - 'Yet some still take delight in finding amusement in it,' criticizing those who find pleasure in inappropriate content.
  • 何俗之不古也如是 - 'How unrefined and modern the customs have become,' lamenting the decline in cultural standards.
  • 盖宗吉以褒善贬恶之学 - 'Qu Zongji, with his principles of praising goodness and condemning evil,' describing his moral and literary approach.
  • 训导之间，游其耳目于词翰之场 - 'In teaching, he immersed himself in the world of literature and writing,' indicating his engagement with literary pursuits.
  • 闻见既多，积累益富 - 'Having heard and seen much, his knowledge has grown richer,' emphasizing the depth of his experience.
  • 恐其久而记忆之或忘也 - 'Fearing that over time, he might forget these things,' explaining the motivation behind compiling the tales.
  • 故取其事之尤可以感发、可以惩创者 - 'Therefore, he selected stories that could inspire or serve as a warning,' highlighting the didactic purpose of the work.
  • 汇次成编，藏之箧笥，以自恰悦 - 'He compiled them into a collection, kept them in his chest, for his own pleasure,' describing the personal nature of the work.
  • 此宗吉之志也 - 'This is the intention of Qu Zongji,' summarizing his purpose.
  • 余下敏，则既不知其是，亦不知其非 - 'I, being less intelligent, do not know whether it is right or wrong,' expressing humility and uncertainty.
  • 不知何者为可取，何者为可讥 - 'I do not know what is praiseworthy and what is laughable,' acknowledging the complexity of the work.
  • 伏而观之，但见其有文、有诗、有歇、有词、有可喜、有可悲、有可骇、有可嗤 - 'Upon reading it, I see that it contains prose, poetry, essays, lyrics, things to be pleased with, things to be sad about, things to be frightened by, and things to be laughed at,' describing the diverse content.
  • 信宗吉于文学而又有余力于他著者也 - 'It is clear that Qu Zongji is skilled in literature and has the ability to write other works as well,' praising his literary talent.
  • 宗吉索余题，故为赋古体一首以复之云 - 'Qu Zongji asked me to write a preface, so I composed an ancient-style poem in response,' explaining the purpose of the preface.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 昌黎韩子 - Refers to Han Yu, a prominent Tang dynasty writer and scholar from Changli.
  • 柳子厚 - Refers to Liu Zongyuan, another prominent Tang dynasty writer and philosopher.
  • 《毛颖传》 - A famous work by Han Yu, a satirical biography of a brush.
  • 《谪龙说》 - A work by Liu Zongyuan, a story about an exiled dragon.
  • 《河间传》 - Another work by Liu Zongyuan, a biography of a river intendant.
  • 马孺子 - A reference to Ma Ruzi, a writer known for his strange tales.
  • 《剪灯新话》 - The title of the work being discussed, which is a collection of strange tales.
  • 瞿宗吉 - The author of *New Tales of the Lamp Snipping*.
  • 褒善贬恶之学 - A principle of praising goodness and condemning evil, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.

Proper Nouns

  • 昌黎韩子 - Han Yu, a prominent Tang dynasty writer and scholar from Changli.
  • 柳子厚 - Liu Zongyuan, another prominent Tang dynasty writer and philosopher.
  • 《毛颖传》 - A famous work by Han Yu, a satirical biography of a brush.
  • 《谪龙说》 - A work by Liu Zongyuan, a story about an exiled dragon.
  • 《河间传》 - Another work by Liu Zongyuan, a biography of a river intendant.
  • 马孺子 - A reference to Ma Ruzi, a writer known for his strange tales.
  • 《剪灯新话》 - The title of the work being discussed, which is a collection of strange tales.
  • 瞿宗吉 - The author of *New Tales of the Lamp Snipping*.

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 山阳才人 - Talented individuals from the Shanyang region.
  • 与侣 - With companions or alongside friends.
  • 开口为今阖为古 - A paradoxical statement suggesting that the same mouth can speak of both the present and the past, implying the fluidity of time or perspective.
  • 春以桃花染性情 - A metaphorical expression meaning that spring influences one's temperament through the beauty of peach blossoms.
  • 秋将桂子熏言语 - A metaphorical expression meaning that autumn influences one's speech through the fragrance of osmanthus flowers.
  • 感离抚遇心怦怦 - A poetic expression describing the emotional turmoil of feeling both separation and encounter, causing the heart to beat rapidly.
  • 道是无凭还有凭 - A paradoxical statement suggesting that something seems to have no basis but actually does, or that appearances can be deceiving.
  • 沉沉帐底昼吹笛 - A vivid image of someone playing a flute quietly in a dimly lit tent during the day.
  • 煦煦窗前宵剪灯 - A vivid image of someone trimming a lamp warmly by the window at night.
  • 倏而晴兮忽而雨 - A metaphorical expression describing sudden changes in mood or circumstances, like the unpredictable weather.
  • 悲欲啼兮喜欲舞 - A poetic expression describing the intense emotional swings of wanting to cry in sadness and dance in joy.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 山阳 - A historical place name, often associated with cultural and literary significance.
  • 桃花 - Peach blossoms, symbolizing beauty and the arrival of spring.
  • 桂子 - Osmanthus flowers, symbolizing the fragrance and beauty of autumn.
  • 笛 - A traditional Chinese musical instrument, often associated with melancholic or reflective music.
  • 灯 - A traditional Chinese lamp, often used in literary contexts to symbolize warmth and comfort.

Proper Nouns

  • 山阳 - A historical place name, often associated with cultural and literary significance.
  • 桃花 - Peach blossoms, symbolizing beauty and the arrival of spring.
  • 桂子 - Osmanthus flowers, symbolizing the fragrance and beauty of autumn.
  • 笛 - A traditional Chinese musical instrument, often associated with melancholic or reflective music.
  • 灯 - A traditional Chinese lamp, often used in literary contexts to symbolize warmth and comfort.

[7]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 玉萧倚月吹凤凰，金栅和烟锁鹦鹉 - Vivid poetic description of a scene with a jade flute, moonlight, phoenix, golden bars, and smoke trapping a parrot.
  • 一念才荫方寸移 - Metaphorical phrase suggesting a single thought can change one's inner self; '方寸' refers to the heart or mind.
  • 善善恶恶苟无失 - Emphasizes the importance of moral behavior; '善善恶恶' means doing good and avoiding evil.
  • 怪怪奇奇将有之 - Suggests that strange and unusual things may come to pass, reflecting the unpredictable nature of life.
  • 丈夫未达虎为狗 - Metaphor implying that a man who has not achieved his goals may be reduced to a lowly state, like a tiger becoming a dog.
  • 濯足沧浪泥数斗 - Metaphor from the *Laozi* describing washing one's feet in clear water, symbolizing the purification of the soul.
  • 气寒骨耸铮有声 - Describes a person who is cold and rigid, with a sharp and metallic voice.
  • 脱帻目光如电走 - Describes someone removing their hat and looking around with eyes that shine like lightning, suggesting a sharp and intense gaze.
  • 道人青蛇天动摇 - Metaphor referring to a Taoist practitioner moving like a green snake in the sky, symbolizing agility and grace.
  • 下斩寻常花月妖 - Metaphor for cutting down ordinary 'flower and moon demons,' symbolizing mundane distractions or temptations.
  • 茫茫尘海沤万点 - Metaphor describing the vast sea of dust with countless bubbles, symbolizing the transient nature of life.
  • 落落云松酒半瓢 - Metaphor describing a lone pine tree in the clouds with half a gourd of wine, symbolizing solitude and simplicity.
  • 世间万事幻泡耳 - Philosophical statement that all things in the world are like bubbles, emphasizing the impermanence of life.
  • 往往有情能不死 - Suggests that sometimes emotions or passions can endure, even in the face of impermanence.
  • 十二巫山谁道深 - Metaphor referring to the Twelve Peaks of Wushan, symbolizing the depth of human emotions or experiences.
  • 云母屏凤薄如纸 - Describes a cloud-mother screen as thin as paper, symbolizing fragility and delicacy.
  • 莺莺宅前芳享述 - Refers to the house of Yingying from the *Romance of the Western Chamber*, describing the beauty of the place.
  • 燕燕楼中明月低 - Describes a low-hanging moon in a building where Yan Yan resides, creating a romantic and melancholic atmosphere.
  • 从来松柏有孤操 - States that pines and cypresses have always had a solitary and upright character, symbolizing steadfastness and integrity.
  • 不独鸳鸯能并栖 - States that not only can mandarin ducks live together, but other creatures can also do so, emphasizing the universality of companionship.
  • 久在钱塘江上住 - Indicates that the author has lived by the Qiantang River for a long time, showing a personal connection to the place.
  • 厌见潮来又潮去 - Expresses weariness of seeing the tides come and go, symbolizing the repetitive and cyclical nature of life.
  • 燕子衔春几度回 - Describes swallows bringing spring back multiple times, symbolizing the passage of time and the return of seasons.
  • 断梦残魂落何处 - Describes broken dreams and fragmented spirits, suggesting a sense of loss and disorientation.
  • 还君此编长啸歌 - Means the author returns the book to the reader and sings a long song, possibly expressing a sense of completion or closure.
  • 便欲酌以金叵罗 - Describes the author's desire to drink from a golden cup, symbolizing a moment of joy or celebration.
  • 醉来呼枕睡一觉 - Describes the author falling asleep after drinking, symbolizing a moment of rest or escape.
  • 高车驷马游南柯 - Describes a journey in a luxurious carriage, possibly a metaphor for a dream or a fleeting experience.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 造化 - Refers to the natural order or fate, a common concept in Chinese philosophy and literature.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 善文亦以白衣坐于殿角 - Shanwen, dressed in plain clothes, sits at the corner of the hall; describes his humble appearance.
  • 特为三王而设斯会 - Especially for the three kings, this gathering is set up; indicates the exclusivity of the event.
  • 虽江汉之长，川泽之君，咸不得预席 - Even the leaders of the Yangtze and Han rivers, and the kings of lakes and marshes, cannot participate in the banquet; highlights the high status of the participants.
  • 彼白衣而末坐者为何人斯 - What kind of person is that one dressed in plain clothes sitting at the end?; rhetorical question expressing surprise and curiosity.
  • 乃敢于此唐突也 - How dare he be so bold?; expresses indignation at the perceived disrespect.
  • 吾构灵德殿，请其作上梁文，故留之在此尔 - I built the Lingde Hall and asked him to write the beam-lifting text, so I kept him here; explains why Shanwen is present.
  • 文士在座，汝乌得多言 - With a scholar present, why are you saying so much?; rebuke to the speaker for speaking out of turn.
  • 姑退 - Retreat for now; polite dismissal.
  • 赤餫公乃赧然而下 - Redyuan Gong then blushed and stepped down; describes the reaction of the speaker after being rebuked.
  • 有美女二十人，摇明珰，曳轻裾 - Twenty beautiful women, shaking their bright pendants and dragging their light skirts; describes the appearance and movements of the dancers.
  • 于筵前舞凌波之队，歌凌波之词 - Dancing in front of the banquet, performing the 'Lingbo' dance, singing the 'Lingbo' lyrics; describes the performance.
  • 若有人兮波之中，折杨柳兮采芙蓉 - If there is someone in the waves, picking willows and plucking lotus flowers; line from the poem, describing a scene in the water.
  • 振瑶环兮琼佩，璆锵呜兮玲珑 - Shaking jade rings and crystal pendants, ringing and tinkling melodiously; describes the sounds of the dancers' accessories.
  • 衣翩翩兮若惊鸿，身矫矫兮如游龙 - Dressed gracefully like a startled wild goose, their bodies agile like a swimming dragon; describes the dancers' movements.
  • 轻尘生兮罗袜，斜日照兮芳容 - Light dust rises under their silk socks, the slanting sun illuminates their beautiful faces; describes the dancers' appearance.
  • 蹇独立兮西复东，羌可遏兮不可从 - Standing alone, they move back and forth, their grace is uncontainable and untraceable; describes the dancers' fluid movements.
  • 忽飘然而长往，御泠泠之轻凤 - Suddenly they float away, riding on the cool, light wind; describes the dancers' departure.

  • 露何为兮沾裳？风何为兮吹鬓？ - Questions why the dew should wet the clothes and the wind should blow through the hair, expressing a sense of wonder or frustration.
  • 棹歌起兮彩袖挥 - Means 'the oarsman's song rises, and the colorful sleeves are waved,' describing a lively and festive scene.

  • 张莲叶兮为盖，缉藕丝兮为衣 - Means 'spreading lotus leaves as a canopy, weaving lotus threads into clothing,' a poetic description of using natural elements for practical purposes.
  • 日欲落兮风更急，微烟生兮淡月出 - Means 'the sun is setting, and the wind is getting stronger; a light smoke rises, and a faint moon appears,' describing a transition from day to night.
  • 早归来兮难久留，对芳华兮乐不可以终极 - Means 'return early, it is hard to stay long; enjoying the beauty, happiness cannot be fully realized,' expressing a desire to return home and the transient nature of joy.
  • 击灵鼍之鼓，吹玉龙之笛 - Describes the sounds of a magical turtle drum and a jade dragon flute, adding a mystical and enchanting atmosphere to the scene.

  • 东西北三神，共捧一觥 - Means 'the three gods of east, west, and north, together holding a cup,' a symbolic gesture of unity and respect.
  • 致善文前曰 - Means 'presented to the good man before him,' indicating a formal and respectful introduction.
  • 吾等僻处遐陬，不闻典礼，今日之会，获睹盛仪 - Means 'we live in remote corners, unaware of ceremonies; today's gathering allows us to witness a grand ceremony,' expressing gratitude and awe.
  • 而又幸遇大君子在座，光采倍增 - Means 'and it is fortunate to have a great gentleman present, greatly enhancing the splendor,' expressing admiration and respect.
  • 不知可乎？ - Means 'do you not think so?' or 'may we do so?' expressing a request for permission or approval.
  • 善文不可辞，遂献水宫庆会诗二十韵 - Means 'the good man could not refuse, and thus presented a poem celebrating the gathering in twenty rhyming couplets,' indicating a formal and poetic response.
  • 帝德乾坤大，神功岭海安 - Means 'the imperial virtue is vast as heaven and earth, the divine power ensures peace in the mountains and seas,' praising the emperor's benevolence and the gods' protection.
  • 渊宫舟栋宇，水路息波澜 - Means 'the deep palace has boats and buildings, the waterways are calm,' describing a peaceful and well-ordered underwater realm.
  • 列爵王侯贵，分符地界宽 - Means 'titles and ranks of nobility are bestowed, territories are wide and expansive,' describing the hierarchical structure and vastness of the underwater realm.
  • 威灵闻赫羿，事业保全完 - Means 'the divine power is known far and wide, achievements are complete and secure,' praising the power and success of the underwater realm.
  • 南极常通奏，炎方永授官 - Means 'messages from the southern pole are always conveyed, officials are appointed in the southern regions,' describing the communication and governance of the underwater realm.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 生而质钝，不通诗书 - Born with a dull nature, unable to understand poetry and books, indicating a lack of natural intelligence or literary skills.
  • 家颇丰殖，以田庄为业 - The family was quite wealthy, engaged in managing estates, indicating a prosperous household.
  • 同里有缪君者，除得闽中一官，缺少路费，于自实处假银二百两 - There was a Mr. Miao in the same village, who was appointed to an official position in Fujian, lacked travel expenses, and borrowed two hundred taels of silver from Mr. Zizhi.
  • 不问其文券，如数贷之 - Did not ask for a written document, lent the full amount, indicating trust and lack of formal documentation.
  • 至正末，山东大乱，自实为群盗听劫，家计一空 - At the end of the Zhizheng era, Shandong was in chaos, Mr. Zizhi was robbed by a band of thieves, and the family's wealth was completely depleted.
  • 时陈有定据守福建，七闽颇安 - At that time, Chen Youding was holding Fujian, the seven regions of Fujian were relatively peaceful, indicating stability.
  • 自实乃挈奏子由海道趋福州，将访缪君而投托焉 - Mr. Zizhi then took his son and traveled by sea to Fuzhou, intending to visit Mr. Miao and seek his protection.
  • 至则缪君果在有定幕下，当道用事，威权隆重，门户赫弈 - When they arrived, Mr. Miao was indeed serving under Chen Youding, held power and influence, had great authority and prestige, and had a prominent and influential household.
  • 自实大喜，然而患难之余，跋涉道途，衣裳褴缕，客貌憔粹，未敢遽见也 - Mr. Zizhi was very happy, but after the hardships, traveled long and arduous journeys, wore tattered clothes, looked haggard and worn out, and did not dare to see him immediately.
  • 乃于城中僦屋，安顿其妻孥，整饰其冠服，卜日而往 - Then rented a house in the city, settled his wife and children, dressed up his attire, and chose a day to go.
  • 明日，再往，酒果三杯而已，落落无顾念之意，亦不言银两之事 - The next day, he went again, had only three cups of wine and fruit, treated him coldly and without concern, and did not mention the matter of the silver.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 時值季冬 - Formal expression meaning 'it is now the last month of winter.'
  • 已迫新岁 - Means 'the new year is approaching.'
  • 窮居无聊 - Means 'living in poverty and boredom.'
  • 拜且泣曰 - Means 'kneeling and weeping, he said.'
  • 新正在迩 - Means 'the new year is near.'
  • 妻子饥寒 - Means 'my wife and children are suffering from hunger and cold.'
  • 囊乏一钱，瓶无储粟 - Means 'my purse lacks even a single coin, and my jar has no stored grain.'
  • 向者银两，今不敢求 - Means 'the silver I received before, I dare not ask for now.'
  • 但愿捐斗水而活涸辙之枯，下壶飨而救翳桑之饿 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'I only wish to give a measure of water to revive a fish stranded in a dried-up ditch, or offer a pot of food to save someone starving in the shade of a mulberry tree.'
  • 故人之赐也 - Means 'this would be a favor from an old friend.'
  • 伏望怜之悯之，哀之恤之 - Means 'I humbly beg for your pity, compassion, and help.'
  • 遂匍匐于地 - Means 'he then prostrated himself on the ground.'
  • 屈指计日之数 - Means 'counting the days on his fingers.'
  • 当是除夕 - Means 'it will be New Year's Eve.'
  • 君可于家专待 - Means 'you can stay at home and wait.'
  • 吾分禄米二右及银二锭 - Means 'I will send two measures of grain and two ingots of silver.'
  • 令人驰送于宅 - Means 'I will have someone deliver them to your house.'
  • 以为过岁之资 - Means 'as funds for the new year.'
  • 幸勿以少为怪 - Means 'please do not be surprised by the small amount.'
  • 且又再三丁宁 - Means 'and I will repeatedly warn you.'
  • 毋用他出以候之 - Means 'do not leave your home to wait.'
  • 自实感谢而退 - Means 'Self-Realized thanked him and left.'
  • 归以缪君之言慰其妻子 - Means 'he returned home and comforted his wife and children with what Master Miao had said.'
  • 至日，举家悬望 - Means 'on that day, the whole family anxiously awaited.'
  • 自实端坐于床 - Means 'Self-Realized sat calmly on the bed.'
  • 令椎子于里门觇之 - Means 'he sent someone to the gate to watch for the delivery.'
  • 须臾，奔入曰：“有人负米至矣。” - Means 'soon, the messenger ran in and said, 'Someone is carrying grain here!'
  • 急出俟焉 - Means 'he rushed out to meet them.'
  • 则越其庐而不顾 - Means 'but they passed by his house without stopping.'
  • 自实犹谓来人不识其家 - Means 'Self-Realized still thought the deliverers did not recognize his home.'
  • 趋往问之 - Means 'he hurried to ask them.'
  • 则曰：“张员外之馈馆宾者也。” - Means 'they said, 'This is from Mr. Zhang, who is sending it to his guest.'
  • 默然而返 - Means 'he silently returned.'
  • 顷之，稚子又入告曰：“有人携钱来矣。” - Means 'soon after, the young child came in and reported, 'Someone is bringing money!'
  • 急出迓焉 - Means 'he rushed out to greet them.'
  • 则过其门而不入 - Means 'but they passed by his door without entering.'
  • 再住扣之 - Means 'he stopped them and asked.'
  • 则曰：“李县令之赆游客者也。” - Means 'they said, 'This is from County Magistrate Li, who is sending it to his guest.'
  • 怃然而惭 - Means 'he looked dejected and felt ashamed.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 季冬 - The twelfth month of the lunar calendar, the last month of winter.
  • 新岁 - The new year, referring to the Lunar New Year.
  • 窮居无聊 - A common expression describing the hardships of poverty and boredom.
  • 拜且泣曰 - A formal and respectful way of speaking, often used in classical Chinese to show deep emotion and respect.
  • 涸辙之枯 - A metaphor from the *Zhuangzi* (庄子) where a fish is stranded in a dried-up ditch, symbolizing someone in dire need.
  • 翳桑之饿 - A reference to a story in the *Zuo Zhuan* (左传) where a starving person is saved by food offered in the shade of a mulberry tree.
  • 故人之赐也 - A common expression of gratitude for a favor from an old friend.
  • 伏望 - A formal expression of humility and pleading.
  • 怜之悯之，哀之恤之 - Common expressions of pity, compassion, and help.
  • 匍匐于地 - A formal expression of deep respect and submission.
  • 屈指计日之数 - A common expression of counting days.
  • 除夕 - New Year's Eve, the last day of the lunar year.
  • 禄米 - Grain given as part of an official's salary.
  • 过岁之资 - Funds needed to survive the new year.
  • 张员外 - A common title for a wealthy landowner or scholar-official.
  • 李县令 - A common title for a county magistrate.
  • 赆游客者 - A gift given to a guest or traveler.
  • 怃然而惭 - A formal expression of dejection and shame.

Proper Nouns

  • 自实 - The protagonist's name.
  • 缪君 - Master Miao, the person the protagonist visits.
  • 张员外 - Mr. Zhang, a wealthy official.
  • 李县令 - County Magistrate Li.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 如是者凡数度 - Repeated occurrences; literally 'in this manner several times'.
  • 竟绝影响 - Complete disappearance; literally 'finally completely cut off all traces'.
  • 粒米束薪，俱不及办 - Lack of basic necessities; literally 'grains and firewood, none available'.
  • 阴砺白刃，坐以待旦 - Preparation and vigilance; literally 'secretly sharpening a white blade, sitting and waiting for dawn'.
  • 鸡鸣鼓绝，径投缪君之门 - Direct and urgent action; literally 'the rooster crows and the drum stops, directly going to the door of Mr. Miao'.
  • 将俟其出而刺之 - Plan for revenge; literally 'intending to wait for him to come out and stab him'.
  • 晨方未启，道无行人 - Early morning and absence of people; literally 'the morning has not yet opened, the road is empty'.
  • 有奇形异状之鬼数十辈从之 - Supernatural followers; literally 'dozens of ghosts with strange shapes and forms following him'.
  • 或握刀剑，或执椎凿 - Different types of weapons; literally 'some holding swords and spears, others holding hammers and chisels'.
  • 披头露体，势甚凶恶 - Appearance of the ghosts; literally 'bareheaded and naked, with a very fierce appearance'.
  • 有金冠玉佩之士百余人随之 - Noble entourage; literally 'over a hundred gentlemen with golden crowns and jade pendants following him'.
  • 或击幢盖，或举旌幡 - Ceremonial actions; literally 'some hitting the canopy, others raising the banners'.
  • 和容婉色，意甚安闲 - Demeanor of the entourage; literally 'with gentle demeanor and pleasant expressions, the intention is very calm'.
  • 叵测 - Unpredictability; literally 'unpredictable'.
  • 自实不敢隐，具言 - Honesty and openness; literally 'Zi Shi dared not hide, and spoke in detail'.
  • 缪君之不义 - Miao's wrongdoing; literally 'Mr. Miao's injustice'.
  • 妻子相向而哭 - Scene of family distress; literally 'wife and children cried facing each other'.
  • 宁人负我，毋我负人 - Moral stance; literally 'rather let others wrong me, than I wrong others'.

  • 旌幡 - Banners or flags, often used in processions or as symbols of authority.

  • This parallel structure emphasizes the meticulousness of the law and the fairness of the way.
  • This parallel structure contrasts the strict enforcement of laws with the limited scope of human intelligence.
  • This parallel structure contrasts the fate of the poor with that of those who enter the palace.

  • This clause criticizes the current system of reward and punishment.
  • This clause introduces the author as a humble and poor scholar.
  • This parallel structure uses metaphors to describe the author's difficult and sorrowful life.
  • This parallel structure describes the author's occasional speaking out against injustice and the resulting criticism and regret.

  • 以径尺玛璃盘，盛大珠四颗，光照一室，不用灯烛 - Describes the opulence of the banquet, where '径尺玛璃盘' means a jade plate a foot in diameter, and '盛大珠四颗' means placing four large pearls.
  • 优人献诵乐语，有黄金七宝酒瓮，重十数斤 - Describes the lavish gifts given during the banquet, where '黄金七宝酒瓮' means a golden seven-treasure wine jar weighing several dozen pounds.
  • 即于座上赐之不吝 - Shows the generosity of Xie Tang, where '赐之不吝' means bestowing it upon them without hesitation.
  • 谢后临朝，梦天倾东南 - Describes a dream with symbolic significance, where '梦天倾东南' means dreaming that the sky was tilting to the southeast.
  • 一人擎之，力若不胜，蹶而复起者三 - Describes the struggle to support the sky, where '擎之' means holding it up, and '蹶而复起者三' means falling and rising three times.
  • 己而一日坠地，傍有一人捧之而奔 - Describes the final fall and the last effort to save it, where '坠地' means falling to the ground, and '捧之而奔' means holding it up and running.
  • 觉而遍访于朝，得二人焉，厥状极肖，擎天者文天祥，捧日者陆秀夫也 - Describes the identification of the dream figures, where '擎天者文天祥' means the one holding up the sky was Wen Tianxiang, and '捧日者陆秀夫也' means the one holding up the sun was Lu Shoufu.
  • 遂不次用之 - Shows the empress dowager's decision to promote these individuals, where '不次用之' means promoting them without regard to seniority.
  • 江万里去国，都民送之郭外者以千计，攀辕忍舍去 - Describes the public's emotional response to Jiang Wanli's departure, where '攀辕忍舍去' means clinging to the carriage and reluctant to let go.

  • 千里马二，一驮督府之印，一载制书并随军赏格，以黄帕覆之 - Describes the equipment and symbols of his authority, where '一驮督府之印' means one carrying the seal of the governor's office, and '一载制书并随军赏格' means the other carrying the imperial edict and military reward chart.

  • 逸念上舍自言生于嘉熙丁酉，至今则百有四十岁矣，而颜貌不衰，言动详雅，止若五六十者，岂有道之流欤 - Phrase reflecting the theme of longevity and wisdom; '上舍自言' means 'the person at the inn said,' '生于嘉熙丁酉' means 'born in the year Ding You of Jiaxi,' '百有四十岁' means 'one hundred and forty years old,' '颜貌不衰' means 'appearance has not aged,' '言动详雅' means 'speech and actions are refined and elegant,' '止若五六十者' means 'as if only fifty or sixty years old,' and '岂有道之流欤' means 'could they be a person of Dao?'
  • 滕穆醉游聚景园记 - Title of a literary work; '滕穆' is the name of the author, '醉游' means 'drunkenly wandering,' and '聚景园' is the name of the garden.
  • 延佑初，永嘉滕生名穆，年二十六，美风调，善吟咏，为众所推许 - Biographical information; '延佑初' means 'in the early years of the Yanyou era,' '永嘉' is a place name, '滕生名穆' means 'Teng Mu,' '美风调' means 'beautiful demeanor and temperament,' '善吟咏' means 'skilled in poetry,' and '为众所推许' means 'highly praised by many.'
  • 素闻临安山水之胜，思一游焉 - Phrase expressing a desire to visit a beautiful place; '素闻' means 'have always heard,' '临安' is a place name, '山水之胜' means 'the beauty of mountains and rivers,' and '思一游焉' means 'thinking to visit once.'
  • 甲寅岁，科举之诏兴，遂以乡书赴荐 - Historical context; '甲寅岁' means 'the year of Jia Yin,' '科举之诏兴' means 'the imperial examination was held,' and '遂以乡书赴荐' means 'he then went to take the exam.'
  • 至则侨居涌金门外，无日不往于南北二山及湖上诸剎 - Describes the author's residence and daily activities; '至则' means 'upon arrival,' '侨居' means 'temporarily reside,' '涌金门外' means 'outside the Yongjin Gate,' '无日不往' means 'went every day,' '南北二山' means 'the two mountains north and south,' and '湖上诸剎' means 'various temples by the lake.'

  • 石屋之洞，冷泉之亭，幽涧深林，悬崖绝壁 - List of specific locations known for their natural beauty; '石屋之洞' means 'the cave of Shiwu,' '冷泉之亭' means 'the pavilion of Lengquan,' '幽涧深林' means 'deep valleys and dense forests,' and '悬崖绝壁' means 'steep cliffs and sheer rocks.'
  • 足迹殆将遍焉 - Phrase indicating extensive travels; '足迹殆将遍焉' means 'his footsteps almost covered everything.'
  • 七月之望，于曲院赏莲，因而宿湖，泊雷峰塔下 - Describes a specific event; '七月之望' means 'the full moon of July,' '曲院' means 'Quyuan,' '赏莲' means 'admire lotus flowers,' '因而宿湖' means 'and so stayed by the lake,' and '泊雷峰塔下' means 'anchored under Leifeng Pagoda.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 是夜 - Refers to 'that night,' a common temporal marker in Classical Chinese.
  • 月色如昼 - Literally 'the moonlight is like daylight,' an idiomatic expression describing a bright moonlit night.
  • 荷香满身 - Literally 'the fragrance of lotus fills the body,' an idiomatic way of saying the air is filled with the scent of lotus flowers.
  • 时闻大鱼跳掷于波间 - Literally 'one occasionally hears large fish leaping in the waves,' an idiomatic description of the sound of fish jumping in the water.
  • 宿鸟飞鸣于岸际 - Literally 'birds that have settled fly and call by the shore,' an idiomatic way of describing birds flying and calling near the shore.
  • 寝不能寐 - Literally 'sleep but cannot sleep,' an idiomatic expression meaning to be unable to fall asleep.
  • 披襟而起 - Literally 'unfasten one's robe and rise,' an idiomatic expression meaning to get up from bed.
  • 绕堤观望 - Literally 'walk around the embankment and look around,' an idiomatic way of describing a leisurely stroll.
  • 信步而入 - Literally 'enter at one's leisure,' an idiomatic expression meaning to enter without a specific destination in mind.
  • 惟瑶津西轩岿然然独存 - Literally 'only the Western Pavilion of the Jade Spring remains standing,' an idiomatic expression emphasizing the enduring nature of the pavilion.
  • 望之殊若神仙 - Literally 'looking at her, she seems like a fairy,' an idiomatic way of describing someone as exceptionally beautiful or ethereal.
  • 屏息以观其所为 - Literally 'hold one's breath and watch what she does,' an idiomatic expression meaning to watch intently.
  • 时移世换 - Literally 'time has changed and the world has transformed,' an idiomatic expression referring to significant changes over time.
  • 令人有《黍离》之悲尔 - Literally 'it makes one feel the sorrow of the *Shu Li*,' an idiomatic reference to a feeling of loss or nostalgia, with *Shu Li* being a poem from the *Book of Songs*.
  • 湖山如故 - Literally 'the lakes and mountains remain unchanged,' an idiomatic expression meaning that the natural scenery has not changed.
  • 风景不殊 - Literally 'the scenery is no different,' an idiomatic expression reinforcing the idea that the landscape has remained the same.
  • 征歌调《玉树》 - Literally 'sing songs in the style of *Yushu*,' an idiomatic expression referring to singing traditional songs.

  • 生亦留神注意，彼此目成久之 - This phrase can be read as 'the person also pays close attention, and they looked at each other for a long time,' emphasizing a mutual focus and connection.
  • 已而酒尽出肆，怏怏登舟，如有所失 - This sequence can be read as 'soon after the wine was finished, they left the shop, boarded the boat with a sense of discontent, as if something were lost,' describing a series of actions and emotions.
  • 是夜遂梦至肆中，入门数重，直抵舍后，始至女室，乃一小轩也 - This clause can be read as 'that night, he dreamed of reaching the shop, entering through several doors, directly reaching behind the house, and finally arriving at the woman's room, which was a small pavilion,' detailing the dream's progression.
  • 轩之前有葡萄架，架下凿池，方圆盈丈，甃以文石，养金鲫其中 - This clause can be read as 'in front of the pavilion, there was a grape trellis, under which a pond was carved, circular and square, a full zhang in size, lined with decorative stone, and containing golden carp,' describing the detailed layout of the pavilion.
  • 池左右植垂丝桧二株，绿荫婆娑，靠墙结一翠柏屏，屏下设石假山三峰，岌然竞秀 - This clause can be read as 'on both sides of the pond, two weeping junipers were planted, their green shade swaying, against the wall, a screen of green cypress was formed, under which three peaks of stone artificial mountains were placed, towering and competing in beauty,' detailing the garden's layout.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 不能聊生 - Means 'unable to sustain oneself,' emphasizing the dire situation of the protagonist.
  • 叩之即有聞 - Means 'to ask and receive an answer,' implying that the deity will provide guidance.
  • 求之无不获 - Means 'to seek and never fail to obtain,' suggesting that the deity will grant all requests.
  • 遑遑汲汲 - Compound idiom meaning 'anxious and eager,' describing a state of constant worry and striving.
  • 冬暖而愁寒 - Means 'warm in winter but still worried about cold,' indicating a persistent sense of insecurity.
  • 年丰而苦饥 - Means 'abundant in harvest but still suffering from hunger,' highlighting the paradox of having enough but still feeling inadequate.
  • 出无知已之投 - Means 'no place to go when leaving home,' indicating a lack of support or recognition outside one's immediate environment.
  • 处无蓄积之守 - Means 'no savings to rely on when staying at home,' emphasizing the lack of financial security.
  • 妻孥贱弃 - Means 'wife and children are looked down upon,' indicating social ostracism.
  • 乡党绝交 - Means 'neighbors and friends have cut ties,' further emphasizing social isolation.
  • 困阨艰难 - Means 'in dire straits and facing hardships,' describing the protagonist's overall difficult situation.
  • 无所告诉 - Means 'no one to turn to for help,' indicating a sense of helplessness.

  • 掌发迹之权 - Means 'holding the power to elevate one's fortunes,' indicating the deity's ability to change one's fate.

  • 困鸟托一枝之安 - Metaphorical phrase from the *Book of Zhuangzi* meaning 'a trapped bird finds a branch to rest on,' symbolizing a temporary refuge or solution.

  • 府君 - The title '府君' was commonly used for local magistrates or officials in ancient China, particularly during the Tang and Song dynasties.

  • 判官 - Officials who assist the magistrate in administrative tasks, often depicted in folklore and literature as subordinate but important figures.
  • 发迹司 - A fictional or administrative department in this context, likely responsible for managing records of official advancement or merit.
  • 朝天使 - Envoys sent by the central government to local officials, often to convey imperial decrees or to oversee local administration.
  • 原价 - The concept of 'original price' reflects the idea of fairness and honesty in transactions, particularly in the context of charitable acts.
  • 饘粥 - A simple food given to the poor, symbolizing compassion and relief in times of hardship.
  • 延寿三纪 - The idea of extending life by 36 years is a symbolic reward in Chinese culture, often associated with acts of great virtue or merit.
  • 赐禄万锺 - A generous reward, symbolizing recognition and appreciation for one's contributions to society.

Proper Nouns

  • 发迹司 - A fictional or administrative department in this context, likely responsible for managing records of official advancement or merit.
  • 朝天使 - Envoys sent by the central government to local officials.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 岂生灵寡福，当此涂炭乎？ - Rhetorical question meaning 'Are the living beings lacking in good fortune, to be in such a state of suffering?' It expresses a sense of despair and questioning fate.
  • 抑运数已定，莫之可逃乎？ - Rhetorical question meaning 'Or is it that fate is already decided, and there is no escape from it?' It suggests a sense of inevitability.
  • 众皆颦蹙相顾曰：“非所知也。” - Phrase meaning 'All present frowned and looked at each other, saying, 'We do not know.' It conveys a sense of collective confusion or helplessness.
  • 遂各散去。 - Simple phrase meaning 'So they all dispersed.' It indicates a conclusion to the scene.
  • 友仁始于案下匍匐而出，拜述厥由。 - Phrase meaning 'Friend Ren crawled out from under the table, bowed, and explained the cause.' It describes a humble and respectful action.
  • 君后大有福禄，非久于贪困者，自兹以往，当日胜一日，脱晦向明矣。 - Phrase meaning 'You will have great fortune and wealth in the future, not staying long in poverty. From now on, each day will be better than the last, moving from darkness to light.' It conveys a message of hope and improvement.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 吏宣旨诘之曰 - The official announced the imperial edict and questioned him; '宣旨' means 'to announce the imperial edict,' and '诘' means 'to question.'
  • 汝为一方神祗，众所敬奉 - You are a local deity, revered by many; '一方' means 'local,' and '神祗' means 'deity.'
  • 奈何辄以威祸恐人，求其祀飨 - Why do you constantly threaten people with calamity to demand worship? '辄' means 'constantly,' and '祀飨' means 'worship and offerings.'
  • 迫此儒士，几陷死地 - You have pressured this scholar, nearly causing his death; '迫' means 'to pressure,' and '几陷死地' means 'nearly causing death.'
  • 贪婪苦虐，何所逃刑 - Your greed and cruelty, where can you escape punishment? '贪婪' means 'greed,' '苦虐' means 'cruelty,' and '逃刑' means 'escape punishment.'
  • 某实永州野庙之神也 - I am indeed the deity of a wild temple in Yongzhou; '实' means 'indeed,' and '野庙' means 'wild temple.'
  • 然而庙为妖蟒所据，已有年矣 - However, the temple has been occupied by a venomous snake for many years; '为...所据' means 'occupied by,' and '已有年矣' means 'for many years.'
  • 力不能制，旷职已久 - I have been unable to control it, and have neglected my duties for a long time; '力不能制' means 'unable to control,' and '旷职' means 'neglecting duties.'
  • 向者驱驾风雨，邀求奠酹，皆此物所为 - Previously, I would summon wind and rain, demanding sacrifices, all of which were done by this creature; '驱驾' means 'summon,' and '奠酹' means 'sacrifices.'
  • 非某之过 - It is not my fault; '非...之过' means 'not...fault.'
  • 事既如此，何不早陈 - If things are as you say, why didn't you report it earlier? '事既如此' means 'if things are as you say,' and '何不早陈' means 'why didn't you report it earlier.'
  • 此物在世已久，兴妖作孽，无与为比 - This creature has existed for a long time, causing chaos and evil, unmatched by others; '兴妖作孽' means 'causing chaos and evil,' and '无与为比' means 'unmatched by others.'
  • 社鬼祠灵，承其约束 - Local spirits and temple deities, all submit to its commands; '社鬼祠灵' means 'local spirits and temple deities,' and '承其约束' means 'submit to its commands.'
  • 神蛟毒虺，受其指挥 - Mythical dragons and poisonous snakes, all follow its orders; '神蛟毒虺' means 'mythical dragons and poisonous snakes,' and '受其指挥' means 'follow its orders.'
  • 每欲奔诉，多方抵截，终莫能达 - Whenever I try to report it, it obstructs me in many ways, and I cannot reach anyone; '每欲奔诉' means 'whenever I try to report it,' '多方抵截' means 'obstructs me in many ways,' and '终莫能达' means 'cannot reach anyone.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 吏宣旨诘之曰 - The official is acting on behalf of the emperor, indicating a hierarchical and authoritative context.
  • 汝为一方神祗，众所敬奉 - Local deities were often venerated in Chinese culture, especially in rural areas.
  • 奈何辄以威祸恐人，求其祀飨 - The practice of demanding sacrifices and offerings from believers is a common theme in Chinese religious texts.
  • 迫此儒士，几陷死地 - Scholars were highly respected in Chinese society, and their protection was often emphasized.
  • 贪婪苦虐，何所逃刑 - Greed and cruelty were often condemned in Chinese moral and legal texts.
  • 某实永州野庙之神也 - Yongzhou is a historical region in China, and wild temples were common in rural areas.
  • 然而庙为妖蟒所据，已有年矣 - The concept of temples being occupied by supernatural entities is a common motif in Chinese folklore.
  • 力不能制，旷职已久 - The neglect of duties by deities or officials is a recurring theme in Chinese literature.
  • 向者驱驾风雨，邀求奠酹，皆此物所为 - The summoning of natural phenomena by deities is a common theme in Chinese mythology.
  • 非某之过 - The deity is trying to shift blame, which is a common human behavior.
  • 事既如此，何不早陈 - The official is questioning the deity's failure to report the issue earlier.
  • 此物在世已久，兴妖作孽，无与为比 - The description of a long-standing and powerful supernatural entity causing chaos is a common motif in Chinese folklore.
  • 社鬼祠灵，承其约束 - Local spirits and temple deities were often depicted as subservient to more powerful supernatural entities.
  • 神蛟毒虺，受其指挥 - Mythical creatures were often depicted as following the orders of more powerful beings.
  • 每欲奔诉，多方抵截，终莫能达 - The difficulty of reporting issues to higher authorities is a common theme in Chinese literature.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The long sequence '须臾，及门，有二红灯前导，为首者顶三山冠，绛帕首，披淡黄袍，束玉带，径据神案而坐' must be read as a single, detailed description of the scholar's arrival and the appearance of the leading figure.
  • The clause '从者十余辈，各执器仗，罗列阶下，仪卫虽甚整肃，而状貌则皆豭貜之类也' must be read as a single, descriptive statement about the followers and their appearance.
  • The sequence '生知为邪魅，取腰间箭，持满一发，正中坐者之臂，失声而走，群党一时溃散，莫知所之' must be read as a series of linked events describing the scholar's actions and the reaction of the evil spirits.
  • The clause '久之，寂然，乃假寐待旦' must be read as a transition in the narrative, describing the passage of time and the scholar's subsequent actions.
  • The long sequence '则见神座边鲜血点点，从大门而出，沿路不绝，循山而南，将及五里，得一大穴，血踪由此而入' must be read as a continuous narrative describing the discovery of the blood trail and the cave.
  • The sequence '生往来穴口，顾盼之间，草根柔滑，不觉失足而坠，乃深坑万仞，仰不见天，自分必死，旁边微觉有路，寻路而行，转入幽遽，咫尺不辨' must be read as a series of linked events describing the scholar's fall and subsequent exploration.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 生，聪敏者也 - Classical descriptive phrase meaning 'He was a clever and intelligent person.'
  • 承上接下，咸得其欢 - Means 'He treated those above and below him with respect and gained their favor.'
  • 代书回简，曲尽其意 - Means 'He wrote letters and replies that fully conveyed his thoughts.'
  • 将军大以为得人，待之甚厚 - Means 'The general greatly thought he had found a capable person and treated him very well.'
  • 但欲一达其意，而终无便可乘 - Means 'He only wanted to convey his feelings, but there was no opportunity to do so.'
  • 荏苒数月，时及授衣 - Means 'Months passed, and it was time for the autumn clothes to be given out.'
  • 西风夕起，白露为霜 - A poetic description of the changing seasons, meaning 'The west wind blew at dusk, and the white dew turned to frost.'
  • 独处空斋，终夜不寐 - Means 'He stayed alone in an empty room, unable to sleep all night.'
  • 好花移入玉阑干，春色无缘得再看 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'The beautiful flowers were moved into the jade railings, and the spring scenery could no longer be seen.'
  • 乐处岂知愁处苦，别时虽易见时难 - A philosophical statement meaning 'In moments of joy, one does not know the pain in moments of sorrow; it is easy to part but hard to meet again.'
  • 何年塞上重归马？此夜庭中独舞鸾 - A rhetorical question and metaphor meaning 'When will the horse return from the frontier? This night, the phoenix dances alone in the courtyard.'
  • 雾阁云窗深几许？可怜辜负月团圆 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'How deep are the misty halls and clouded windows? It is pitiful to miss the full moon.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 可怜辜负月团圆 - A metaphor for missing out on a moment of happiness or completeness.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The phrase '生，聪敏者也' introduces the main character and his qualities.
  • The clause '承上接下，咸得其欢' describes how the character interacts with others.
  • The clause '代书回简，曲尽其意' explains the character's skill in writing.
  • The clause '将军大以为得人，待之甚厚' describes the general's reaction to the character.
  • The clause '但欲一达其意，而终无便可乘' expresses the character's desire to communicate but the lack of opportunity.
  • The clause '荏苒数月，时及授衣' sets the time frame and the season.
  • The clause '西风夕起，白露为霜' provides a vivid description of the changing seasons.
  • The clause '独处空斋，终夜不寐' describes the character's state of mind.
  • The clause '好花移入玉阑干，春色无缘得再看' uses a metaphor to express the passage of time.
  • The clause '乐处岂知愁处苦，别时虽易见时难' reflects on the duality of life's experiences.
  • The clause '何年塞上重归马？此夜庭中独舞鸾' uses a rhetorical question and metaphor to express longing and loneliness.
  • The clause '雾阁云窗深几许？可怜辜负月团圆' uses a metaphor to express the depth of emotions and the missed opportunity for happiness.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 伏以 - Formal introductory phrase meaning 'bowing to' or 'submitting to,' used to begin a respectful address or statement.
  • 父生母育 - Literally 'father gives birth, mother nurtures,' but in classical usage, it refers to the mutual care and support between parents, often used metaphorically for reciprocal obligations.
  • 难酬罔极之恩 - A phrase expressing the difficulty of repaying boundless or infinite kindness. '罔极' (boundless) is a common expression for something immeasurable, especially in the context of parental love.
  • 夫唱妇随 - A phrase meaning 'the husband leads, the wife follows,' referring to the traditional Confucian ideal of harmonious marital relationships.
  • 夙着三从之义 - Refers to the 'Three Obediences' in Confucian ethics: a woman's obedience to her father before marriage, to her husband after marriage, and to her son in widowhood.
  • 在人伦而已定 - Means 'already established in human relationships,' referring to the fixed roles and duties within society.
  • 何时事之多艰 - A rhetorical question meaning 'how many times have things been difficult?' or 'how often have there been hardships?' It emphasizes the frequent challenges faced.
  • 曩者 - A temporal adverb meaning 'formerly' or 'in the past.'
  • 汉日将颓 - A metaphorical expression indicating the decline of the Han dynasty, where '汉日' (Han sun) symbolizes the Han era, and '将颓' (on the verge of collapse) suggests its impending fall.
  • 楚氛甚恶 - Refers to the chaotic and hostile atmosphere of the Chu state, which was often in conflict with the Han. '楚氛' (Chu atmosphere) symbolizes the turbulent and dangerous conditions.
  • 倒持太阿之柄 - A metaphor for misusing power, where '太阿' (Tai'e) is a legendary sword, and '倒持' (holding upside down) implies a reversal of proper authority.
  • 擅弄潢池之兵 - Refers to unauthorized military actions, where '潢池' (Huángchí) is a metaphor for a small pond, suggesting that the actions were insignificant yet dangerous.
  • 豕长蛇 - A metaphor for chaotic and destructive forces, combining the imagery of pigs and snakes, which were considered pests in ancient Chinese culture.
  • 互相吞并 - Means 'mutually devouring' or 'engaging in mutual conquest,' describing the destructive nature of conflict.
  • 雄蜂雌蝶 - A metaphor for different types of people or forces, where '雄蜂' (male bees) and '雌蝶' (female butterflies) represent contrasting characteristics, possibly symbolizing strength and grace.
  • 各自逃生 - Means 'each trying to save themselves,' indicating a situation where individuals prioritize their own survival over collective action.
  • 不能玉碎于乱离 - A metaphor for not being able to die honorably in chaos, where '玉碎' (crushing jade) symbolizes a noble and dignified death.
  • 乃至瓦全于仓卒 - A metaphor for surviving in a chaotic situation, where '瓦全' (preserving pottery) symbolizes a less honorable but more pragmatic survival.
  • 驱驰战马，随逐征鞍 - Describes the chaotic scene of fleeing on horseback, where '驱驰' (driving) and '随逐' (following) emphasize the urgency and disorder.
  • 望高天而八翼莫飞 - A metaphor for feeling powerless, where '八翼' (eight wings) symbolizes the desire to escape, but '莫飞' (cannot fly) indicates the impossibility of doing so.
  • 思故国而三魂屡散 - A metaphor for longing for one's homeland, where '三魂' (three souls) symbolizes the fragmented and scattered state of the mind.
  • 良辰易迈 - Means 'good times easily pass,' emphasizing the fleeting nature of favorable circumstances.
  • 伤青鸾之伴木鸡 - A metaphor for the loss of companionship, where '青鸾' (blue phoenix) and '木鸡' (wooden chicken) represent noble and humble companions, respectively.
  • 怨偶为仇 - Means 'a once-loving couple becomes enemies,' describing a tragic transformation in a relationship.
  • 惧乌鸦之打丹凤 - A metaphor for fear of harm, where '乌鸦' (crow) and '丹凤' (peacock) represent ordinary and noble entities, respectively.
  • 虽应酬而为乐，终感激而生悲 - A contrast between superficial enjoyment and deeper sorrow, where '应酬' (socializing) and '感激' (gratitude) highlight the difference between outward behavior and inner feelings.
  • 夜月杜鹃之啼 - A metaphor for sorrowful cries, where '杜鹃' (cuckoo bird) is often associated with mourning in Chinese literature.
  • 春风蝴蝶之梦 - A metaphor for fleeting dreams, where '春风' (spring wind) and '蝴蝶' (butterfly) symbolize the ephemeral nature of beauty and joy.
  • 时移事往 - Means 'times change and events pass,' emphasizing the passage of time.
  • 苦尽甘来 - Means 'after suffering comes sweetness,' a common Chinese idiom expressing hope after hardship.
  • 杨素览镜而归妻 - A reference to Yang Su, a prominent figure during the Sui dynasty, who, according to legend, looked into a mirror and saw his wife, symbolizing reunion after separation.
  • 王敦开合而放妓 - A reference to Wang Dun, a military leader during the Eastern Jin dynasty, who, according to legend, opened a door and released his concubines, symbolizing liberation.
  • 蓬岛践当时之约 - A metaphor for fulfilling a promise, where '蓬岛' (Penglai Island) is a mythical paradise, and '践' (fulfill) emphasizes the importance of keeping one's word.
  • 潇湘有故人之逢 - A metaphor for meeting an old friend, where '潇湘' (Xiao and Xiang rivers) is a poetic reference to a place of reunion, and '故人' (old friend) emphasizes the significance of the encounter.

  • 自怜赋命之屯 - Self-pitying phrase meaning 'grieving over one's own fate,' expressing a sense of misfortune or hardship.
  • 不恨寻春之晚 - Means 'not resenting the late arrival of spring,' suggesting a philosophical acceptance of timing.
  • 章台之柳，虽已折于他人；玄都之花，尚不改于前度 - A metaphorical expression comparing the state of affairs to the condition of willows on Changtai and flowers in Xuandu, implying that while some things have changed, others remain constant.
  • 将谓瓶沉而簪折，岂期壁返而珠还 - A metaphorical phrase meaning 'one might have thought the vase would sink and the hairpin would break, but who would have expected the wall to return and the pearl to be restored,' suggesting unexpected reversals of fortune.
  • 殆同玉萧女两世姻缘，难比红拂妓一时配合 - A comparison to the two-life marriage of Yu Xiao Nu and the one-time union of Hong Fu, highlighting the rarity and uniqueness of certain relationships.
  • 天与其便，事非偶然 - Means 'heaven has granted convenience, and the matter is not by chance,' suggesting that events are divinely ordained.
  • 煎鸾胶而续断弦，重谐缱绻 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to melt phoenix glue and mend the broken strings, to rekindle the tender bond,' referring to the act of repairing a broken relationship.
  • 托鱼腹而传尺素，谨致丁宁 - Means 'to entrust a letter to a fish's belly and carefully convey the message,' referring to the act of sending a letter.
  • 未奉甘旨，先此申复 - Means 'before fulfilling the duty to serve one's parents, I first express this response,' indicating a sense of urgency or importance.

  • 其父即赁舟与仆自淮徂浙，径奔吴兴 - Means 'the father immediately rented a boat and traveled with the servant from Huai to Zhejiang, directly heading to Wuxing,' describing a swift journey.
  • 至道场山下畴昔留宿之处，则荒烟野草，狐兔之迹交道，前所见屋宇，乃东西两坟耳 - Means 'upon arriving at the place where they had stayed the night before, they found it overgrown with wild grass and fox and rabbit tracks, and the buildings they had seen before were now two graves,' describing a scene of decay.
  • 方疑访间，适有野僧扶锡而过，叩而问焉 - Means 'just as they were wondering and looking around, a wild monk passed by with his staff, and they asked him,' describing a chance encounter.
  • 此故李将军所葬金生与翠娘之坟耳，岂有人居乎？ - Means 'this is the grave of Jin Sheng and Cui Niang, the general's former companions, not a place where people live,' clarifying the nature of the site.
  • 大惊 - Means 'greatly surprised,' expressing a strong reaction.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 至今过者指为金、翠墓云 - Could mean 'Today, those who pass by point to it as the tomb of Jin and Cui' or 'Today, those who pass by believe it is the tomb of Jin and Cui'.
  • 或以为右崖陡出，若塘岸焉 - Could mean 'some say the right cliff protrudes steeply, like a pond bank' or 'some say the right cliff is steep, like a pond bank'.
  • 其地左吴淞而右太湖 - Could mean 'the place is on the left of Wusong and on the right of Taihu Lake' or 'the place is surrounded by Wusong on the left and Taihu Lake on the right'.
  • 风涛险恶 - Could mean 'wind and waves are dangerous' or 'the wind and waves are treacherous'.
  • 众水听汇 - Could mean 'all waters converge' or 'all waters flow together'.
  • 过者必致敬于庙庭而后行 - Could mean 'those who pass by must pay respect at the temple before proceeding' or 'those who pass by must show respect at the temple before continuing'.
  • 夙着灵异 - Could mean 'long known for its spiritual marvels' or 'long renowned for its supernatural occurrences'.
  • 具载于范石湖所编《吴郡志》 - Could mean 'all recorded in Fan Shihu's compilation of *Wu County Gazetteer*' or 'all documented in Fan Shihu's *Wu County Gazetteer*'.
  • 元统间 - Could mean 'during the Yuantong era' or 'in the Yuantong period'.
  • 闻生子述者，以歌诗鸣于吴下 - Could mean 'there was a man named Zishu who became famous for his songs and poems in Wu' or 'there was a man named Zishu who was known for his songs and poems in Wu'.
  • 因过其处 - Could mean 'because he passed by the place' or 'because he happened to be there'.
  • 适值龙挂 - Could mean 'just happened to encounter a dragon hanging' or 'just happened to see a dragon hanging'.
  • 乃白龙也 - Could mean 'it was a white dragon' or 'it turned out to be a white dragon'.
  • 毊鬣下垂 - Could mean 'with long hair hanging down' or 'with long hair drooping'.
  • 如一玉住 - Could mean 'like a piece of jade' or 'like a jade statue'.
  • 鳞甲照耀 - Could mean 'scales shining brightly' or 'scales glowing brightly'.
  • 如明镜数百片 - Could mean 'like hundreds of pieces of bright mirrors' or 'like hundreds of bright mirrors'.
  • 转侧于乌云之内 - Could mean 'turning and moving within dark clouds' or 'moving within dark clouds'.
  • 良久而没 - Could mean 'for a long time before disappearing' or 'for a long time before vanishing'.
  • 子述自以为平生奇观 - Could mean 'Zishu thought it was the most extraordinary sight of his life' or 'Zishu considered it the most extraordinary sight of his life'.
  • 莫之能及 - Could mean 'no one could match it' or 'no one could equal it'.
  • 雨止，登庙 - Could mean 'when the rain stopped, he climbed the temple' or 'when the rain ceased, he climbed the temple'.
  • 周览既毕 - Could mean 'after thoroughly viewing everything' or 'after viewing everything thoroughly'.
  • 乃题古风一章于庑下曰 - Could mean 'he then wrote a chapter of ancient style poetry under the eaves, saying' or 'he then composed a chapter of ancient style poetry under the eaves, saying'.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 忽闻 - Literary expression meaning 'suddenly hear' or 'suddenly come to hear.'
  • 佛像鼓腹而笑 - Vivid and somewhat unusual description of a Buddha statue laughing with its belly, metaphorical and difficult to translate literally.
  • 彼求之而不得，吾不求而自至 - Philosophical statement suggesting that something sought by others is unattainable, but what is not sought comes naturally.
  • 今夜好顿点心，不用食斋也 - Colloquial expression meaning 'it's a good time for a snack tonight, no need to fast,' culturally specific and difficult to translate directly.
  • 振迅而起 - Vivid description of the statue moving quickly, possibly with a sense of sudden energy or vigor.
  • 其行甚重 - Means 'its movement is very heavy' or 'it moves with great weight.'
  • 蹴然仆地 - Means 'it fell suddenly to the ground,' with '蹴然' emphasizing the suddenness.
  • 土木狼籍，胎骨糜碎 - Vivid description of the statue being broken into pieces, with '狼籍' meaning 'in disorder' and '胎骨' referring to the internal structure.
  • 胡鬼弄汝公，反自掇其祸矣 - Colloquial expression meaning 'the barbarian ghost played with your master, and in turn, it brought its own disaster,' culturally specific and difficult to translate directly.
  • 踉蹡哮吼 - Vivid description of the ghosts moving unsteadily and roaring, with '踉蹡' meaning 'stumbling' and '哮吼' meaning 'roaring.'
  • 搏牛粪而掷，或攫人骨而投 - Vivid and somewhat grotesque description of the ghosts throwing cow dung and human bones, with '搏' meaning 'to grab' and '攫' meaning 'to seize.'
  • 无头者则提头以趁之 - Means 'those without heads would carry their heads to chase after him,' with '趁' meaning 'to chase' or 'to pursue.'

  • 佛像 - Refers to a Buddha statue, a common religious symbol in Chinese culture.

  • 食斋 - Refers to fasting, a practice often associated with religious or spiritual observance.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '忽闻佛像鼓腹而笑曰' introduces the Buddha statue speaking, which is a literary device and may be difficult to translate directly.
  • The sentence '彼求之而不得，吾不求而自至' contains a philosophical contrast that may be difficult to translate directly.

  • 笑拍阑干扣玉壶 - A vivid, idiomatic expression combining actions ('laughing,' 'clapping,' 'knocking') and objects ('railing,' 'jade vase') to evoke a scene of playful interaction.
  • 林鸦惊散渚禽呼 - A poetic description of birds scattering in alarm, using natural imagery to convey a sense of disruption.
  • 一江流水三更月 - A concise, evocative phrase combining a natural scene ('river,' 'moon') with a temporal marker ('third watch of the night') to create a serene yet solitary atmosphere.
  • 两岸青山六代都 - A compact phrase that juxtaposes natural beauty ('green mountains') with historical significance ('six dynasties'), suggesting a connection between the landscape and the past.
  • 富贵不来吾老矣 - A resigned statement about aging and the absence of wealth or status.
  • 幽明无间子知乎 - A philosophical question about the relationship between the visible and invisible worlds, implying a deeper understanding beyond surface appearances.
  • 旁人若问前程事 - A hypothetical scenario where someone asks about future prospects.
  • 积善行仁是坦途 - A moral maxim emphasizing that doing good and practicing benevolence leads to a smooth path in life.
  • 满身风露夜茫茫 - A vivid description of being drenched in dew and mist under a vast, dark night sky.

  • 铁瓮城边人玩月 - A scene of people enjoying the moon by the Iron Pot City, a historical reference to a fortified city.
  • 鬼门关外客还乡 - A metaphorical expression suggesting a return home after a perilous journey, possibly alluding to death or a near-death experience.
  • 功名不博诗千首 - A statement contrasting the pursuit of fame and success with the value of literary creation.
  • 生死何殊梦一场 - A philosophical reflection on the transient nature of life and death, likening them to a dream.
  • 赖有故人知此意 - An expression of gratitude for a friend who understands one's intentions.
  • 清谈终夕据藤床 - A description of engaging in intellectual conversation late into the night on a bamboo bed.
  • 太上立德，其次立功。其次立言 - A classic Confucian triad emphasizing the highest virtue (moral character), followed by achievement (honor), and finally, words (literary legacy).
  • 仆在世之日，无德可称，无功可述 - A humble statement about lacking notable moral or practical achievements.
  • 然而着成集录，不下数百卷 - A modest claim about having written a substantial collection of works.
  • 作为文章，将及千余篇 - Another modest claim about producing a large number of literary pieces.
  • 皆极深研几，尽意而为之者 - A statement about the depth and intentionality of the author's work.
  • 奄忽以来，家事零替 - A description of sudden changes leading to the decline of one's family affairs.
  • 内无应门之童，外绝知音之士 - A lament about the absence of household help and intellectual companions.
  • 盗贼之所攘窃，虫鼠之所毁伤 - A description of losses due to theft and natural decay.
  • 十不存一，甚可惜也 - A statement about the extensive loss and regret.
  • 伏望故人以怜才为念，恤交为心 - A plea for a friend to show compassion and care for the writer.
  • 捐季子之宝剑，付尧夫之麦舟 - Metaphorical expressions requesting a friend to provide material support, invoking historical figures.
  • 用财于当行，施德于不报 - A statement about using resources wisely and practicing kindness without expecting returns.
  • 刻之桐梓，传于好事 - A request to preserve the works on durable materials and share them with those who appreciate them.
  • 庶几不与草木同腐此则故人之赐也 - A hope that the works will endure and not decay like grass and trees, attributing this to the friend's generosity.
  • 兴言及此，惭愧何胜！ - A statement of deep gratitude and humility.
  • 友人许诺。颜大喜，捧觞拜献，以致丁宁之意 - A description of the friend's agreement and the author's joyful response, including a formal gesture of thanks.

  • 将有诚悃，籍卿传之于世耳 - Expression of trust: 'There is something sincere that I wish to entrust to you to pass on to the world.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 罔知忌惮 - Classical expression meaning 'without fear or restraint,' used to describe a state of recklessness.
  • 是可忍也，孰不可忍 - Proverb from the *Analects* of Confucius, meaning 'If this can be endured, what cannot be endured?' It is a rhetorical question expressing extreme indignation at the lack of moral boundaries.
  • 鹊桥之会 - Refers to the mythological meeting of the Cowherd (牵牛, Qian Niu) and the Weaver Girl (织女, Zhi Nu) on the Qixi Bridge in the sky, symbolizing love and reunion.
  • 牛渚之游 - Refers to a legendary boat trip on the Niu Zhi (牛渚, Ox Ford) area of the Yangtze River, possibly alluding to a romantic or adventurous encounter.
  • 嫦娥月殿之奔 - Refers to the myth of Chang'e (嫦娥), the Moon Goddess, who fled to the Moon Palace after stealing the elixir of immortality.
  • 神女高唐之会 - Refers to the story of the Fairy of the High Tang (高唐神女), a legendary encounter between a mortal and a celestial being in the High Tang region.
  • 后土灵佑之事 - Refers to the legendary events involving Hou Tu (后土), the Earth Deity, known for her protective and nurturing qualities.
  • 湘灵冥会之诗 - Refers to the poetry about Xiang Ling (湘灵), the goddess of the Xiang River, and her mysterious encounters in the afterlife.
  • 大禹开峡之功 - Refers to the legendary achievement of Yu the Great (大禹) in opening up the Three Gorges of the Yangtze River.
  • 巫神实佐之 - Means 'the shamanic deities actually assisted him,' referring to the divine help Yu received during his labor.
  • 尧女舜妃 - Refers to the daughters of Emperor Yao and the consorts of Emperor Shun, emphasizing their noble lineage.
  • 圣贤之裔 - Means 'descendants of sages and virtuous people,' indicating a lineage of moral and intellectual excellence.
  • 贞烈之伦 - Means 'those of chaste and heroic character,' describing individuals of high moral integrity.
  • 世俗所谓 - Means 'what the common people say,' contrasting with the actual historical or mythological accounts.
  • 上元之降封陟 - Refers to the myth of the Jade Emperor (上元) descending to bestow titles and honors.
  • 云英之遇裴航 - Refers to the story of Yun Ying (云英) meeting Pei Hang (裴航), a romantic tale of a fairy and a mortal.
  • 兰香之嫁张硕 - Refers to the story of Lan Xiang (兰香) marrying Zhang Shuo (张硕), another romantic tale of a fairy and a mortal.
  • 彩鸾之配文箫 - Refers to the story of Cai Luan (彩鸾) marrying Wen Xiao (文箫), yet another romantic tale of a fairy and a mortal.
  • 情欲易生 - Means 'passions are easily aroused,' referring to the ease with which romantic feelings can arise.
  • 事迹难掩 - Means 'the facts are hard to conceal,' indicating that the romantic encounters are well-documented and widely known.
  • 嫦娥应悔偷灵药，碧海青天夜夜心 - A line from Li Bai's poem, meaning 'Chang'e should regret stealing the elixir, with her heart in the blue sea and clear sky every night.'
  • 一自高唐赋成后，楚他天云雨尽堪疑 - A line from a poem, meaning 'Since the *Gao Tang Fu* (High Tang Ode) was written, all the clouds and rains in Chu can be doubted.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 罔知忌惮，是可忍也，孰不可忍 - This sentence is a rhetorical question expressing extreme indignation at the lack of moral boundaries.
  • 鹊桥之会，牛渚之游，今听神言，审其妄矣 - This sequence of events is described in a compact and somewhat ambiguous manner, requiring careful interpretation to maintain the intended meaning.
  • 嫦娥月殿之奔，神女高唐之会，后土灵佑之事，湘灵冥会之诗，果有之乎，抑未然乎 - This list of events and stories is presented in a way that requires careful parsing to ensure clarity in English.
  • 嫦娥应悔偷灵药，碧海青天夜夜心 - This line from Li Bai's poem is a metaphorical expression that requires a nuanced translation to capture the poetic imagery.
  • 一自高唐赋成后，楚他天云雨尽堪疑 - This line from a poem is a metaphorical expression that requires a nuanced translation to capture the poetic imagery.

  • 大禹 - Yu the Great.

  • 孤眠霜宿侮之乎 - Rhetorical question implying that the sun and moon, being celestial bodies, should not be subjected to such human-like insults or degradations.
  • 云者，山川灵气，雨者，天地沛泽 - Describes clouds as the spirits of mountains and rivers, and rain as the abundant grace of heaven and earth.
  • 因宋玉之谬，辄指为房帷之乐 - Refers to the misinterpretation by Song Yu, who incorrectly associated clouds and rain with marital pleasure.
  • 慢伸渎天，莫此为甚 - Critiques the disrespectful and sacrilegious act of equating natural phenomena with human desires.
  • 湘君夫人，帝舜之配 - Refers to the Lady of Xiang, the wife of Emperor Shun, a figure from Chinese mythology.
  • 陟方之日，盖已老矣 - Indicates that the time of Emperor Shun's ascension to the throne was long ago, and the Lady of Xiang was already old.
  • 李群玉者，果何人欤 - Rhetorical question questioning the identity and credibility of Li Qunyu, a poet.
  • 敢以淫邪之词，溷于黄陵之庙 - Critiques the audacity of using lewd and inappropriate language in the context of the Yellow Emperor's temple.
  • 不知精爽落何处，疑是行云秋色中 - Poetic expression suggesting that the spirit of the Lady of Xiang might be found among the clouds and autumn scenery.
  • 韦郎年少眈闲事，案上休看《太白经》 - Playful verse suggesting that a young man named Wei should not read the *Taibai Jing* (a text on alchemy) as it is not suitable for his age.
  • 欲界诸天，皆有配耦，其无耦者，则无欲者也 - States that all beings in the desire realm have partners, and those without partners are those who have no desires.
